system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: Jazz Standard Study #3: All of Me
Pages 53-69 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

In many cases, the order of these jazz standards studies is intentional.
Autumn Leaves started you out with some core harmonic principles
that you need to understand. Blue Bossa reinforced those harmonic
principles and explored a departure from one diatonic key center.

Our next jazz standard, All of Me by Gerald Marks and Seymour Si-
mon, starts to drift into more complex diatonic harmony with multiple
different ways to perceive it. We'll talk more about that in the chords

analysis, but first...
At of Me Bie

All of Me was written by Gerald Marks and Seymour Simon in 1931. It has been
widely recorded and has become one of the most popular jazz standards of all
time. It is most commonly played in the key of Concert C major.

All of Me has a 32 bar form and is usually played with a swing feel. Its
melody is relatively simple.

When learning this song, there are many different versions to choose
from. | encourage you to listen to as many as you can. But here are
some good ones to start with.

All of Me is a popular song amongst vocalists and one reason for this
is it has a great set of lyrics. | encourage you to learn the lyrics from
one of the recordings | listed above. As | mentioned at the beginning
of this book, learning the lyrics to a jazz standard can be both helpful
in internalizing the melody of a song and understanding the essence
behind it.


>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Frank Sinatra from “Frank Sinatra Sings His Greatest
Hits” 1951

Billie Holiday from “Best of Billie Holiday” 2015

Louis Armstrong from “Louis Armstrong Sings” 1932

Oscar Peterson from “A Jazz Portrait of Frank
Sinatra” 1959

Now let’s start taking a look into All of Me. First, take a look at the
chord map, then play through the guide tone chart. After that, take a
look at the chord analysis, and I'll start unpacking some of the differ-
ent harmonic structures.

As l’ve said before, there are different ways to analyze harmony. This
is just one way, and what | believe to be the most straightforward way.
As was the case with Autumn Leaves, most of the chords in All of Me
can be related to its parent key center. But that is not necessarily the
best way to understand it.

First, let’s look at what the color-coded sections represent:
Blue = C major (parent key)

‘Yellow =D minor (or ii chord to parent key)
Red = Aminor (or vi chord to parent key)

Green = C minor (a brief jump to the iv minor of parent key)

Here’s something important that | want you to understand about har-
mony:

To understand the function of a chord, you must look at both the
chord that comes before it and after it.

Let’s look at the first 8 bars.

cé E7
I Chord in C Major - - - ------------ II7inD Minor --------------------
(Tonic)
A’ Duin’
* — VChord in D Minor ---------------- iChordinD Minor -----------------
(AKA V of ii) ii Chord of Tonic

The form starts in the parent key center for the first two bars. Bars
3-8 | consider being in the key of D minor, which related to the parent
key would be the ii chord. The E7 is acting as a dominant || chord in
D minor. As | briefly mentioned in our Autumn Leaves study, jazz

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



musicians often turn diatonic functioning minor chords into dominant
7“ chords. This is quite common. Considering the next chord is an A7,
and then a Dmin/, it becomes clear that the E7 and A7 are targeting
the key of D minor. At the same time, it’s important to understand
how each of these chords individually relates to the parent key. For
example, A7 is the dominant VI to the parent key.

The next 8 bars can be best seen as moving into a new key center as
well.

E’

Amin?

7 VChord inAMinor ---------------- i Chord inAMinor -----------------

D’

(AKA V of vi) (AKA vi of Tonic)

Duin’ G’

* II Dominant of C Major - --- ---------- ii-V of C Major ___-----------------

Now we are back at the E7, but it’s important to note the following
chord, Amin7. In this case, it becomes clear that the E7 is no longer
functioning as a dominant || chord, but a V chord resolving to a minor i
chord. You can think of this as a V of vi, since Amin7 is the vi chord to
the parent key.

This is important because you will see this happen more throughout
the rest of our studies. When you see a chord labeled “V of vi” it is
what we call a secondary dominant.

>> WHAT'S A SECONDARY DOMINANT?

A secondary dominant is when a dominant 7 chord acts as a V chord
of a diatonic chord other than the tonic. We call this “tonicization.” This
means the chord the secondary dominant precedes now sounds like a
new tonic to the listener.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 62 |



If this seems confusing at first, look back at bars 9-12 and study the
relationship.

The next 4 bars are commonly seen in jazz standards. The harmonic
movement lingers on the dominant II for a few bars before converting
back to its original minor quality and functions in a ii-V back to the top
of the form (or second A section).

There’s one more section worth looking at. Let’s talk about it.

(Fain? B’? Emin?)
Fe Fuin® Cua’ A’
# IV Chord In C Major _ iv Minor I VI
(Quick Jump to Key OF C Minor)

In bar 25 we start with the IV chord. It transitions to a iv minor chord,
which you could analyze as a jump into the key of C minor. Note that
| do list some alternative chord changes above what | would consider
the “basic” ones. Essentially you can start a cycle of fourths from the
iv minor.

| personally like to move to the Emin7 chord in bar 27, which would be
the iii chord to the parent key. It creates more harmonic movement
which can be enjoyable when improvising.

Don’t get too hung up on the iv minor chord. In your improvisation, you
will simply want to consider the difference in the chord quality. Don’t
be playing major thirds or major 7ths!

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 63 |



IMPROV LESSON
Using Chord Tones in Your Folos

During our Autumn Leaves study, we talked about guide tones which
we have been including in all of our jazz standard studies. In our Blue
Bossa study, we talked about using the melody in your solo. Like a
puzzle, we are gradually starting to fill in the missing pieces.

This improv lesson is all about using chord tones in your solos. We have
seen chord tones in our chord maps thus far, but this lesson is going to
dig deeper.

>> WHATIS A CHORD TONE?

A chord tone is a note derived from a chords related scale and makes
up the basic structure of the chord. The basic chord tones in any 7"
chord are: Root-3-5"-7", The 3", 5" and 7" are altered depending on
the quality of the chord.

It’s vital that you understand how to build major 7, minor 7, dominant
7, half-diminished, and diminished 7 chords. For the purposes of this
book, | don’t want to go too deep into that. If you lack this basic un-
derstanding, | would encourage you to check out my jazz theory book
Zero to Improv which goes over this in much more depth.

But let’s quickly go over the formulas for these basic 7* chords.


>> 7™ CHORD FORMULAS:

Formula for a major 7 chord: Root-3"-5"-7%
Formula for a dominant 7 chord: Root-3"-5'-b7
Formula for a minor 7 chord: Root-b3-5"-b7
Formula for a half-diminished chord: Root-b3-b5-b7
Formula for a diminished 7 chord: Root-b3-b5-bb7

The good news is we already have identified the most important chord
tones, which are the guide tones. Continue to let the 3rds and 7ths of
each chord be your guide. But let’s start utilizing these other ones as
well, because ultimately over and above scales we want to be impro-
vising off of these.

All of Me is a great song to explore this on because even the melody
itself utilizes the chord tones quite extensively. There are extensions
and altered extensions (13*, b9...etc) in the melody as well, but the
focus is around the basic chord tones.

Let’s take a look at the chord tones within the basic melodic structure
of All of Me. There is no recording to accompany this, so just analyze.

| want to give credit to saxophonist and educator Josiah Boornazian
who first brought this to my attention. He’s a brilliant pro musician and
educator who also writes for the Learn Jazz Standards blog.

Go ahead and look through this chart and see if you can independent-
ly verify each of these chord tones and how they relate to the given
chords.

In some cases, you will see a “CA” over a note. This stands for “chro-
matic approach,” meaning the note in question does not represent a
basic chord tone, extension or alteration, but is simply chromatically
approaching a diatonic note. In some cases, they are connecting two
diatonic notes together. It can be helpful to observe all of this, espe-
cially moving on to the next exercise | want to go over.

The exercise you will find on the coming page is a Chord Tone Exer-
cise over All of Me. In this exercise, | have taken a simple rhythmic
figure and limited the note choices to only chord tones of each chord.
To reiterate, the only notes being used are the Root, 3%, 5%, 7** (or 6")
and the variations of, depending on the chord quality.

Of course, this is not the way you would want to improvise in a live play-
ing situation. This is just an exercise. But by limiting yourself to these
notes, you are “mapping out” All of Me and further outlining the chord
changes.

One important thing to note:

The chord tones are played as arpeggios, meaning they always move
in order. However, the first chord tone of a given chord may not always
be the root. | have written this exercise using voice leading as a rule of
thumb. Whatever the last note played in a particular bar was, | move
to the nearest chord tone of the next chord. You'll notice that often
this note is even the same note as the previous bar.

Regardless of which chord tone is started on, they always go in order.
Root-3-5%-7*, or 3rd-5%-7%-Root, or 5%-7%-Root-3"...etc. To help you
identify this, I’ve labeled the chord tone of the first note of each mea-
sure. If you're feeling confused, it will be made clear when you take a
look at the exercise.

Be sure to play through this so you can get the hang of it. Even though
| have given you the first chord tone of each measure, | encourage you
to identify the chord tones for yourself.

Also, take a look at how | transition from one chord to another by al-
ways playing the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord. Again,
this isn’t necessarily musical or how you should improvise, but it helps
us learn how to outline any chord regardless of what chord tone is start-
ed on or which chord came before.

All of Me is a jazz standard that has some interesting harmonic move-
ment, and we can learn a lot from it. Be sure to spend lots of time
practicing this one and making sense out of the chords analysis.

But for now, let’s move on to our next jazz standard study: It Could
Happen to You.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
